label: Yirmeyahu (Jeremiah) — Part 4 — Chapters 24–31
summary_plain: YHWH shows Yirmeyahu two baskets of figs — good figs and bad figs. The good figs are those sent into exile whom YHWH will watch over and bring back. YHWH says: You will seek me and find me when you seek me with all your heart. I will be found by you and restore your fortunes and gather you from all the nations and all the places where I have driven you. Rachel weeps for her children and refuses to be comforted — but YHWH says there is hope for your future. Behold, the days are coming when I will make a new covenant with the house of Yisra'EL and the house of Yehudah — not like the covenant I made with their fathers. I will put my Torah within them, and I will write it on their hearts.
summary_scholarly: The ACR reads from the DSS Jeremiah manuscripts (4QJer-a through 4QJer-e) and the Orit Ge'ez. Yirmeyahu 31:31-34 is the defining covenant passage in the entire prophetic literature — the new covenant (berit hadashah). The DSS preserve this concept in the Damascus Document (CD), which describes the Qumran community as 'those who entered the new covenant in the land of Damascus.' The new covenant is not a replacement of the Torah — it is the Torah written on hearts instead of tablets. The mechanism changes (internal instead of external), but the content remains the Torah. No institutional mediation is required — 'they shall all know me, from the least to the greatest.'

key_terms.definition: YHWH declares in 31:31-34: I will make a new covenant — not like the covenant with the fathers. I will put my Torah within them and write it on their hearts. I will be their Creator, they shall be my people. They shall all know me. The content is the Torah — the mechanism is internalized.

YHWH shows Yirmeyahu two baskets of figs (chapter 24). The good figs are the exiles whom YHWH will watch over and restore. The bad figs are those who remain and refuse correction. The exile is not punishment alone — it is refinement.

Rachel weeps for her children and refuses to be comforted because they are no more (31:15). YHWH responds: 'There is hope for your future — your children shall come back to their own country' (31:17). The mourning is acknowledged; the restoration is promised.

The new covenant's defining feature: 'I will put my Torah within them, and I will write it on their hearts' (31:33). The Torah is not abolished — it is internalized. No stone tablets, no institutional gatekeeper, no mediating priesthood required.

A new set of laws replacing the Torah

multiple_choice.question: Who weeps for her children?
multiple_choice.source_quote: A voice is heard in Ramah — lamentation and bitter weeping. Rachel is weeping for her children — she refuses to be comforted because they are no more.

options: When you seek me with all your heart
multiple_choice.source_quote: You will seek me and find me, when you seek me with all your heart.

faq.question: Does the new covenant of Yirmeyahu 31:31 replace the Torah?
faq.answer: No. The text is explicit: 'I will put my Torah within them, and I will write it on their hearts' (31:33). The content of the new covenant is the Torah — the same Torah given at Sinai. What changes is the mechanism: external tablets become internal inscription. Stone becomes heart. The new covenant does not introduce new content — it internalizes the existing content. Roman Christianity reinterpreted 'new covenant' as the replacement of the Torah with a new dispensation mediated by the Church. This reading contradicts the text itself, which says the Torah is the content of the new covenant. The Damascus Document (CD 6:19, 8:21, 19:33-34) at Qumran describes the community as 'those who entered the new covenant in the land of Damascus' — and their practice was meticulous Torah observance. The oldest interpreters of the new covenant practiced Torah. The institution that claimed the new covenant abolished Torah.

faq.question: What does 'they shall all know me' mean for institutional authority?
faq.answer: Yirmeyahu 31:34: 'No longer shall each one teach his neighbor and each his brother, saying: Know YHWH — for they shall all know me, from the least of them to the greatest, declares YHWH.' This is the elimination of institutional mediation. In the new covenant, knowledge of YHWH is direct — from the least to the greatest, without hierarchy, without a priestly class controlling access, without an ordained ministry dispensing approved interpretation. The Roman Church built an institutional hierarchy — pope, cardinals, bishops, priests — as the required mediator between YHWH and his people. The Rabbinical tradition built a parallel hierarchy of Torah authority — the oral Torah transmitted through an unbroken chain. Both systems contradict the plain text of Yirmeyahu 31:34: they shall ALL know me. No intermediary. No gatekeeper. Direct knowledge, written on the heart.

faq.question: How does the new covenant connect to the Great Eraser?
faq.answer: The new covenant of Yirmeyahu 31:31-34 is the most systematically misappropriated passage in the covenant literature. The Roman Church took the Hebrew term berit hadashah (new covenant), translated it into Greek as kaine diatheke, applied it to a collection of Greek texts written decades to centuries after the events they describe, and called it the 'New Testament.' They then declared the original Hebrew covenant the 'Old Testament' — linguistically demoting the primary text to secondary status. The actual new covenant described by Yirmeyahu is Torah written on hearts, direct knowledge of YHWH without institutional mediation. The Roman appropriation replaced Torah with Church doctrine, replaced direct knowledge with sacramental mediation, and replaced the covenant people with a universal 'Church' that systematically erased the African identity of the original community. The term 'new covenant' was stolen, redefined, and weaponized against the people to whom it was promised.
